data.title: 2 Infrastructure Plays To Power The AI Boom
data.description:
Written by Nick Ackerman

Power is essential to everyday human life, which has never been more important than now in the digital age and, particularly, the rise of artificial intelligence. It requires massive amounts of energy to power the AI infrastructure, more specifically, the data centers that are the 'brains' behind making it all possible. Of course, with no power, that brain becomes completely useless. That is why we've seen a significant rise in the growth trajectory of infrastructure names that can provide power and/or deliver power.

Today, we are looking at two names that provide AI with the essential power but, even without AI, still remain key infrastructure plays over the long term. It is only that the AI boom has been an added catalyst. Further, these are reasonably valued plays, not egregiously overpriced, where they would expect to see a significant collapse whenever the next AI slowdown spook may occur.

#1 DTE Energy (DTE)

Starting off with perhaps a more boring potential investment that investors could consider, it is a somewhat traditional multi-utility company. They operate a distribution network of electricity and gas throughout Michigan. That said, the electric business is by far their largest segment when it comes to earnings, accounting for over 80% of their operating earnings.

They recently announced a deal with Alphabet's (GOOG)(GOOGL) Google to develop 1 GW for their data center, highlighting the potential for keeping earnings growth healthy. Analysts estimate that earnings growth over the coming years should come in between 5% and 9%. EPS growth estimates from DTE themselves show that they are expected to grow 6-8% through 2030.

Shares of DTE have been on a fairly healthy trajectory higher over the last couple of years, which has resulted in the valuations not being quite as tempting as they once were. That said, despite the expectation for healthy growth going forward, they are trading right around their historical P/E.

However, this also suggests that DTE isn't overvalued either. In fact, analysts have an average price target of near $160 for shares of DTE. According to a Morningstar analyst covering this name, they've also recently lifted their fair value estimate to $157. This was fairly meaningful because only a month prior, they had a fair value estimate of $139. The move was driven by the recent Google data center announcement, as well as a rate increase that was required and approved on February 19.

Finally, DTE can be worth owning over the long term because of its healthy and growing dividend. It comes with a dividend yield of 3.15%, which is already quite respectable; however, the 16 years of dividend growth are impressive. Even when they weren't growing their dividend in decades past, they held it consistent for many years.

In 2021, it may look like they reduced their payout, but that was to adjust for their DT Midstream (DTM) spin-off—which has done incredibly well on its own, up over 240% in only a handful of years since.

With that, DTE has seen the last decade with a CAGR of ~6.3%. Given the growing earnings and historical commitment to the dividend, that is only expected to continue going forward. That's a level that generally outpaces inflation in most years, which means it is helping to keep purchasing power growing for investors taking cash or compounding even faster for those that reinvest.

#2 Brookfield Renewable Partners L.P. (BEP)

Looking at a more broad-based infrastructure play rather than a more specifically focused on a relatively geographically specific utility play like DTE, we have BEP. They provide diversified global exposure, partnering with many different companies. Their largest earnings segment comes in the form of hydro at 44%, but they also have solar, wind, and nuclear assets.

This is a limited partnership, which does mean it comes with a K-1 form at tax time. I don't personally find that to be an issue, but some investors may then prefer the alternative, Brookfield Renewable Corporation (BEPC) shares rather than the BEP units. That can avoid the K-1 filing but retains the exact same economics and is exchangeable at the shareholder's option.

One of the downsides of BEPC shares is that you are paying a premium, which has become a bit more substantial in the last year or so. This is evident in the form of looking at the distribution yield of each. BEP's distribution yield is meaningfully higher compared to BEPC's dividend yield, despite the exact same dividend. This is specifically because BEPC's shares trade at a higher price.

At the same time, the counterargument could be made that it is always going to trade at a premium, so if we are looking at relative valuation historically, it isn't that big of a deal. I think that is a valid argument and has some merit, but it will be up to each investor to decide.

In the case of BEP, we have seen its unit price start to rise quite materially from where it was around a year ago. I know it isn't as great a bargain as it was at one time, and one could be more patient to wait for the next pullback here. Personally, I got in around $20 and then added again around $25. Units can be quite volatile, so being more patient to add more aggressively could be warranted.

Still, I think that, relatively speaking, we aren't seeing BEP at any sort of extreme valuation either. That can be backed up a bit by its historical P/FFO and where it is currently trading.

Additionally, while that is today's valuation, earnings here are expected to grow going forward at around ~9% on average in the next three years, according to analysts. BEP itself is targeting 10% FFO per unit growth. This is helped along by things like the $10 billion renewable energy deal signed with Microsoft (MSFT) last year to power its data center. Therefore, what may look like fair value or getting close to its current value may look cheap if an investor has a longer-term outlook of just 1-3 years.

Further, investors are being rewarded with a healthy distribution yield, of over 4.3%—but that's not all; it is set to continue to grow annually going forward.

They are targeting 5-9% growth going forward. This is something they've been able to achieve going back over the last decade; now there is consistent growth in their payout to investors. Over the last 5- and 10-year periods, delivering right around 5.2% annualized growth. To be fair, that is on the low end of what they are targeting going forward. However, I believe that it is still attractive for something that is already relatively higher yielding.

data.title: Palantir Down 31%: Might Nibble Around Earnings
data.description:
Over the past year, I have written four pieces about Palantir Technologies Inc. (PLTR). My conclusion had always been a rather indifferent “Hold” from the sidelines, while the nuanced tone had literally shifted from “anything goes” to slightly more cautious stances as the share price continued to climb until late 2025. Since these articles, PLTR has returned +49%, +3%, -16%, and -26%, averaging +3%, clearly lagging the S&P 500’s (SP500) average +15% measured from the same 4 points in time.

PLTR underperformed despite crushing estimates time and time again, underscoring the fact that valuation does matter sooner or later. A company can be formidable and still underperform as a stock when all its bright future has been priced into the share price previously.

Nevertheless, I have also always been willing to try to understand the price, looking at the company from various quantitative angles and constructing DCFs with highly optimistic 20-year planning periods. Today, I update my models ahead of earnings on Monday, 4 May 2026. I will conclude that I am tempted to nibble a bit ahead of the report. Still, investors are spoiled after 2 and a half years of consecutive beats and consistent growth acceleration. Nothing less is the base case for the next earnings release and must be met.

Turning Into A U.S. Pure Play

Palantir’s top-line growth continued to accelerate quarter after quarter throughout 2025. The 6-year revenue CAGR from 2018 through 2024 had been 30% and climbed to a 7-year CAGR of 33% from the same base year of 2018 through 2025. As is typical for software companies, operational leverage sets in at some critical point of scale. We see this impressively at Palantir, with doubling or tripling operating margins in 2024 and 2025 and increasing unadjusted free cash flow margins from 31% in 2023 to 40% in 2024 and now 47% in 2025. This already slightly exceeds initial assumptions of 45% until 2027 in my first forecast model from February 2025.

Looking under the hood of the top line, we see an ever-increasing U.S. focus. Palantir’s growth acceleration is primarily driven by a U.S. growth explosion to YoY rates as high as 93% in the most recent quarter, while international growth of 24% is still strong but nowhere near its home market. As a result, Palantir’s roughly 50:50 revenue share only three years ago has shifted to 76:24, increasingly rendering it a U.S. pure play. International growth rates would be insufficient to validate Palantir’s valuation, so at least U.S. growth must stay intact.

Palantir guides for $7.2 billion in revenue for FY 2026. That is an incredible $2.4 billion more than analysts had expected for that year 14 months ago and $1 billion more than median analysts’ estimations at the beginning of this year. The 2025 through 2028 CAGR based on median estimates now stands at 49%, up from a 31% 3-year CAGR when I built my first model around PLTR in February 2025. Growth rates for the 7 years thereafter now average 25% annually, and coverage density has increased, with now at least 3 analysts having submitted revenue estimates until 2035, up from only 1 back in February 2025.

Discount Rate Sinks

Against the backdrop of an already reached unadjusted FCF margin of 47%, I am increasing and/or pulling forward FCF margin assumptions to a maximum of 60%. Pulled-forward margin expansion and some absolute increases obviously enhance the company’s value.

For PLTR, I only calculate the cost of equity and use it as WACC, as debt is irrelevant against the company’s cash position and especially its market capitalization. My discount rate for PLTR has decreased from 14-15% to around 12% recently, driven by 1) the significantly increased U.S. share with its lower equity risk premium compared to international and 2) significantly lower beta factors recently. Only U.S. risk-free proxies have increased. My overall lower discount rate also contributes to enhancing my value estimate for PLTR.

Given aggressive increases in mid-term growth assumptions and guidance by the company itself, I am again lowering the terminal growth rate from 7.5% to 5% to compensate for this at the longer end, which, however, is still extremely ambitious. From 2036 through 2045, I let the growth rate gradually decrease towards terminal growth. Note that due to terminal value setting in only 20 years from now in this model, terminal growth is not as sensitive as in models with shorter planning periods.

Half-A-Trillion Dollars

Now, these have, as always, been quite optimistic assumptions. Palantir’s revenue would increase 61-fold over the next 20 years to land at $273 billion, up from $4.5 billion last year. FCF would expand 78-fold over the next 20 years to land at $164 billion, up from $2.1 billion last year. At the discussed discount rate of 12%, this optimistic model would land at an enterprise value of over half a trillion dollars for Palantir, which currently trades at $336 billion. Aggressive growth and margin expansion must continue to justify this, but I am now considering initiating a first small high-risk position at a max 1% portfolio weight.

What You Pay Vs. What You Get

Next, let us again benchmark PLTR against the Magnificent 7 to set into perspective “what you pay” versus “what you get” for each stock. The chart below reaches up to 100% but is cropped at 20% to better visualize FCF yields.

For example, you theoretically “get” $2.1 billion of TTM FCF (green below) from PLTR as an investor and “pay” $336 billion for this. That’s a 0.6% FCF yield. The rest is expected future cash flows. Microsoft (MSFT), on the other hand, currently generates $77.4 billion TTM FCF, for which investors pay an EV of $3.2 trillion. That’s a 2.4% FCF yield.

In this comparison, Tesla (TSLA), for example, represents a worse deal than PLTR, as it only has $7 billion of TTM FCF for its investors, who pay $1.4 trillion for the company. This equates to an FCF yield of only 0.5%. Nvidia (NVDA), on the other hand, trades at a 1.9% TTM FCF yield.

Now how about risk-to-growth relations? Let us put the 8 companies onto a matrix in which the average of 2-year and 5-year raw beta factors represents risk and consensus revenue growth for the ongoing FY represents growth.

Here again, Tesla, for example, offers little tangible revenue growth while the stock remains highly volatile, which represents a poor risk-reward deal from my perspective. PLTR and NVDA, on the other hand, seem to justify much of their heightened volatility with their blowout growth rates. All other Magnificent Seven are in somewhat more moderate areas both in terms of beta and revenue growth.

Leaving Dark Red Behind: Entering The Orange Zone

As I have updated my purely quantitative approaches surrounding one of the most talked about and controversial stocks in the AI space, it seems it came back from the “dark red” RPM area into “orange” territory. The fact that the stock is down 31% from its all-time high, despite exceeding 2026 revenue guidance by a billion dollars, shows that valuation always matters sooner or later. Palantir cannot be an unrestricted Buy at its valuation, also considering increasing competition in the space, albeit first-mover and lock-in advantages are real.

Nevertheless, I am tempted to initiate a small position in PLTR stock, given my extremely optimistic model is for the first time aligning with market pricing. Still, little ought to go wrong with the company’s growth trajectory. Q1 earnings next week (May 4th) will give us the next glimpse into this, with expected revenue growth of 74%, versus 70% YoY in Q4. Numbers should exceed these expectations to support lofty valuations.

data.title: Carillon Scout Mid Cap Fund Q1 2026 Commentary
data.description:
Market Overview

US equity markets were volatile in the first quarter of 2026, as investors weighed still-solid corporate earnings expectations and ongoing artificial intelligence (AI) infrastructure spending against a more complicated geopolitical backdrop. Market leadership broadened early in the quarter, with value stocks outperforming growth and small- and mid-cap equities generally holding up better than the largest technology-oriented benchmarks, though sentiment worsened in March. The war in the Middle East pushed energy prices higher and added to inflation concerns. Meanwhile, the US Federal Reserve left interest rates unchanged at its March meeting, noting that economic activity remained solid, job gains had been modest, and inflation was still somewhat elevated. Overall, the fundamental backdrop was constructive coming into 2026, but elevated valuations and a dangerous geopolitical shock created sharp swings in market leadership and risk appetite.

Portfolio Review

Top securities

Ciena (CIEN) designs and manufactures advanced optical networking hardware, including transport systems and pluggable transceivers. The stock's strong performance was driven primarily by the strength in cloud providers' demand as Ciena's high-speed optical networking solutions are critical components of high-performance computing capacity buildouts. Hyperscalers led the trend while emerging customer groups such as smaller AI-focused cloud data center operators grew rapidly. Additionally, a recovery in telecommunications network investment continues and is expected to extend beyond 2026.

Teradyne (TER) supplies automated test equipment for semiconductors, electronics systems, and wireless devices. It also designs and develops robotic products for manufacturing and warehouse task automations. The company benefits from increasing semiconductor test complexity for AI accelerator, networking, and memory chips. Positive earnings results and an optimistic AI narrative helped push the stock higher. We remain optimistic about Teradyne's AI-driven opportunities, including potential market share gains with graphics processing unit (GPU) and application-specific integrated circuit (ASIC) semiconductor designs.

Marathon Petroleum (MPC), one of the largest independent US refiners with a sizable midstream business, performed well in the first quarter of 2026 as investors responded to a favorable mix of strong underlying cash generation, large capital returns, and a better energy backdrop late in the quarter. Refining stocks benefited from elevated crack spreads caused by disruptions fueled both by the change in leadership in Venezuela and the Iranian conflict, which greatly reduced energy product shipments through the Strait of Hormuz. Short-term performance will likely depend on whether the strait reopens for shipping traffic, but changing energy logistic patterns may boost domestic refiner profitability over the long term.

ATI (ATI) is a specialty materials company that makes high-performance metals and alloys. The company recently exited its commoditized metals business and increased its exposure to higher-margin nickel and titanium alloys. Sales to the aerospace industry now account for more than two-thirds of total revenue, up from less than half just four years ago. An increasing aerospace production outlook, new business awards, improved execution, and a focus on more value-added engine and airframe parts has boosted investor interest. An opportunity in nuclear energy also is emerging. We believe ATI should trade more in line with other aerospace parts suppliers as the company proves it can produce more consistent revenue growth at higher margins.

Casey's General Stores (CASY), one of the largest convenience store operators in the US, performed well in the first quarter as investors responded to another strong earnings report and the resilience of its higher-margin inside sales business. Management highlighted strength in prepared food, beverages, and fuel margins, which reinforced the view that Casey's value-oriented model continued to gain share amid a mixed consumer environment.

Bottom securities

MongoDB (MDB) is a database management company that builds and supports a non-relational database solution. It is simple for developers to learn and use, while still providing the capabilities needed to meet complex requirements at any scale. AI disruption fears caused a significant sell-off in most of the software industry, including MongoDB. A more conservative outlook by new management exacerbated the sell-off as investors had bid the stock up in the second half of 2025, anticipating a strong 2026.

ServiceTitan (TTAN) provides cloud-based software that helps trades businesses manage scheduling, dispatch, billing, marketing, payments, and other back-office workflows. The stock lagged in the first quarter even after reporting solid fiscal fourth-quarter results, as investors focused on AI disruption risk as well as on signs of moderating growth. Management's initial fiscal 2027 outlook was still healthy, but in our view, the market had been positioned for a more forceful acceleration, which contributed to the stock's weakness.

Coupang (CPNG) is a South Korean e-commerce leader, often called the "Amazon of Korea," that provides online retail, food delivery, video streaming, and financial payment services. In November 2025, Coupang disclosed a large cybersecurity breach affecting tens of millions of customer records. South Korean authorities raided Coupang's headquarters, and the CEO of its Korean subsidiary resigned over the incident. This triggered regulatory risk, potential fines, and reputational concerns, which hurt the stock, though it was later discovered that the breach impacted fewer customers than originally reported. Concerns remain that the cybersecurity issue will push customers to other platforms, plus the Iran conflict led to higher energy prices for the Korean economy, which further weighed on the stock.

Agilent Technologies (A) provides analytical instruments, diagnostics, software, services, and consumables used across life sciences, clinical labs, and applied chemical markets. Agilent lagged in the first quarter despite posting solid headline results, as investors focused on a few softer underlying trends. Its Life Sciences and Diagnostics Markets Segment grew below broader expectations, which management credited to weather disruption, continued weakness in academic and government spending, and tariff-related pressure on margins. In our view, the stock lagged because investors were looking for clearer evidence of broad-based demand acceleration rather than a quarter still affected by funding softness and cost headwinds.

Robinhood Markets (HOOD) operates a digital financial services platform that offers commission-free trading in stocks, options, futures, event contracts, and crypto, along with retirement and cash-management products. The stock lagged in the first quarter as investors reacted to a mixed fourth-quarter report, when revenue missed expectations and cryptocurrency transaction revenue fell year over year, reinforcing concerns that one of Robinhood's most important growth drivers had become more volatile. Monthly operating data also showed softer February activity in equities, options, and event contracts versus January, while total platform assets registered a low single-digit decline from the prior month, which likely added to worries about trading momentum after a strong 2025.

Outlook

As we highlighted in our previous outlook, we believe the market had not priced in much economic or geopolitical risk, so we are not surprised by the negative reaction in March to the Iranian conflict. Given the Trump administration's expected duration of the hostilities, we are surprised US equity markets have not declined further. We think this is best explained by the consensus view, which we share, that the closure of the Strait of Hormuz for more than a few weeks would be such a shock to the global economy that a negotiated reopening is highly likely. We expect the strait to experience a slow, steady increase in traffic, which, combined with the rerouting of energy supply chains, may avoid a lengthy energy shock. That said, we acknowledge that the situation is fluid and we will carefully watch for signs that this conflict could endure longer than expected.

Fortunately, the US economy was strong heading into the war. Global food and energy stockpiles were high, and while this provided a buffer to buy time during the conflict, time is running short. For now, the portfolio is overweight to the energy sector and the beta is slightly below 1.0, as we attempt to "ride out the storm" without giving away the upside optionality if a ceasefire is reached.

The key themes in the portfolio have not changed. We have maintained exposure to AI infrastructure holdings as we anticipate large language models will continue to scale, pushing capital expenditures higher than the consensus view. We remain overweight in semiconductors, supported by surging AI token consumption as well as by improving semiconductor demand from the automotive, industrial, and defense industries. We remain selective with AI application software holdings, favoring those supporting the infrastructure layer rather than serving the end user.

We remain constructive on select aerospace suppliers, as rising commercial aerospace build rates are expected, supporting both original equipment manufacturer (OEM) production and aftermarket demand. We acknowledge high jet fuel prices could temporarily slow air travel, though traffic has held up well according to recent airline management updates.

We continue to see potential opportunities in domestic infrastructure – especially energy and electricity – given the growing power needs of data centers and broader electrification trends. We also see opportunities in select consumer companies that offer affordable products, select commodity producers and miners, and life sciences as funding conditions improve and regulatory uncertainty becomes more manageable.

We have grown more cautious on the outlook for the American consumer. While in the near term, higher levels of tax refunds from the One Big Beautiful Bill Act should support spending, we are concerned about the future. Consumers are already dipping into their savings to maintain spending levels. We believe there is risk that AI-related layoff announcements will accelerate over the coming months, placing downward pressure on the consumer broadly. Higher gasoline prices will also hurt consumer spending, especially within the lower-income segment, which is already suffering the brunt of affordability issues.

Overall, stock prices traded lower on energy and inflation fears, which we think created more bargains than were available early in the year. We anticipate energy will find its way out of the Persian Gulf and inflation expectations will moderate. Although a return to normal is unlikely, a reduction in tail risk scenarios may be enough to power US equity markets higher, supported by strong earnings growth and lower risk premiums.

data.title: Novartis: Revenue Lags In Q1, Outlook Unchanged
data.description:
Novartis (NVS) reported first-quarter results today, missing revenue and EPS estimates. I see nothing concerning in the report, as the key growth products continued to perform well and the company reaffirmed the full-year revenue and core operating earnings guidance.

The medium- and long-term outlook remains largely unchanged, but this year's outlook is somewhat lower than I assumed in my October 2025 update, as Entresto net sales in the United States have eroded faster than I expected.

However, I still see a scenario where this year's revenue growth hits the mid-single digits versus the low single-digit guidance by Novartis as products like Kisqali, Kesimpta, Leqvio, and Pluvicto continue to defy gravity.

Novartis also partially addressed my concerns about the lack of meaningful business development activity by acquiring Avidity Biosciences for $12 billion, and the late-stage assets it gained could improve the growth rates toward the end of the decade.

The worst will be over soon

The first quarter of 2026 marks quarter three since Entresto generics launched in the United States.

Entresto net sales in the United States went from $1.22 billion in Q2 2025 to $798 million, $95 million, and $72 million in the next three quarters, respectively, and Q2 2026 will mark the end of tough Y/Y comparisons.

The erosion happened faster than I anticipated. I expected Novartis to be able to grow through this erosion period, and it appears I was wrong as Q1 revenues were down 0.9% Y/Y and Q2 is likely to be another down quarter.

And while that means Novartis lost some tail revenues from Entresto, the good news is the Y/Y comps will become easier sooner than I expected.

Most key products continue to perform well, Cosentyx lags

The part of the earnings report that really matters is the performance of the key growth products. And most performed really well.

Kisqali, Pluvicto, Kesimpta, Scemblix, and Fabhalta continued to gain market share with exceptional growth rates.

I will mention Leqvio separately since it had a nice bump in the first quarter due to the NRDL inclusion in China (government coverage). Y/Y growth rates were down to the 50s in Q3 and Q4 2025, and the bump from China brought it to 69% in the first quarter.

Leqvio is now approaching a $2 billion a year run rate, and that is still just based on the LDL cholesterol-lowering product label. I have high long-term growth expectations of this product, especially after it generates cardiovascular outcomes data next year for secondary prevention and 2-3 years later for primary prevention.

Novartis also highlighted the decent launch of BTK inhibitor Rhapsido for the treatment of chronic spontaneous urticaria. Sales reached $37 million in its second quarter on the market despite the heavy utilization of the free drug program while the company works on securing broader access. Rhapsido is just getting started, and it has true pipeline-in-a-pill potential (more on this in the next section of the article).

The only disappointment in the first quarter among the key products was Cosentyx, but the quarter was better than the numbers show. Total prescriptions in the U.S. increased 6% Y/Y, but sales declined 6% because demand growth was offset by prior positive revenue deduction adjustments.

Clinical data highlights

Pipeline news flow remains light for a company of Novartis' size, and most of the updates were incremental.

The two updates worth highlighting are Rhapsido's (remibrutinib) positive phase 3 results in chronic inducible urticaria (results will be presented at a medical conference later this year) and positive phase 2 results in food allergy.

Rhapsido demonstrated a dose-dependent response to the peanut oral food challenge and did so with rapid onset in adults with IgE-mediated peanut allergy. This was enough for Novartis to start planning a phase 3 trial, which is on track to start in the second half of the year. Novartis estimates Rhapsido could become eligible to treat more than 3.5 million patients across major markets.

An important catalyst for Rhapsido in the second half of the year will be the phase 3 readout in patients with relapsing forms of multiple sclerosis.

Competitor Roche (RHHBY) recently presented positive phase 3 results of its BTK inhibitor fenebrutinib, and while efficacy was really strong with very low annualized relapse rates, liver safety is an ongoing issue, and the trials showed higher death rates in fenebrutinib arms versus placebo, which I expect will limit fenebrutinib's commercial uptake.

Rhapsido has not shown such safety issues in clinical trials to date, and should it show efficacy in the range of fenebrutinib without the safety baggage, it could be positioned as the preferred BTK inhibitor for multiple sclerosis. But success on efficacy is not guaranteed either. Sanofi's (SNY) BTK inhibitor tolebrutinib failed to beat teriflunomide in the phase 3 trial, the same comparator fenebrutinib easily defeated.

The other important clinical updates in the second half of the year will be the cardiovascular outcomes trial results of pelacarsen, phase 3 results of Rhapsido in hidradenitis suppurativa, and the phase 3 results of del-desiran in myotonic dystrophy type 1 ('DM1').

The $12 billion Avidity Biosciences acquisition could have a significant impact on medium- and long-term growth rates

In my October 2025 update, I complained about the lack of meaningful late-stage readouts and the lack of meaningful business development. Novartis addressed the complaint through the $12 billion acquisition of Avidity Biosciences.

The deal brings three assets, two of which are potentially meaningful. However, they are not fully de-risked yet.

Del-desiran’s phase 1/2 data look convincing in myotonic dystrophy type 1 (‘DM1’) patients, but with a huge caveat of being compared to historical controls rather than placebo.

Del-brax did generate good efficacy trends against placebo in the phase 1/2 trial in patients with facioscapulohumeral muscular dystrophy (‘FSHD’), but these were trends in a small number of patients.

These two products are projected to generate around $5 billion in combined annual sales by the early 2030s, and the most bullish projections call for $8-$9 billion.

The first test for the acquired candidates will come in the second half of the year as Novartis expects to report topline results from the phase 3 trial of del-desiran in DM1 patients.

The market is starting to look past Entresto's erosion

I argued last year that it will be difficult for Novartis' valuation multiples, represented by the EV/revenue chart below, to reach new highs and expand in the face of Entresto's erosion. I was wrong, and it seems the market is already looking past the hurdle. And it should, as a return to stronger growth looks inevitable, and it should be further amplified by product portfolio and pipeline expansion through business development.

data.title: Thrivent Large Cap Growth Fund Q1 2026 Commentary
data.description:
Performance factors

Thrivent Large Cap Growth Fund underperformed the Russell 1000 Growth Index in 1Q26. Security selection detracted from results, while allocation contributed modestly to performance. The Information Technology, Health Care, and Financials sectors were the top detractors from performance. AppLovin (APP) was the largest source of underperformance. AppLovin provides advertising software that helps businesses reach consumers, primarily through mobile games. The company's stock underperformed due to rising concerns about emerging artificial intelligence (AI) driven competition. Also, within the Information Technology sector, Shopify, ServiceNow, and Apple detracted from performance. Industrials, Communication Services, and Energy sectors were the strongest contributors to performance. Within Industrials, Caterpillar (CAT) and Fastenal (FAST) drove the gains. Caterpillar, a manufacturer of heavy machinery and power-generation turbines, outperformed due to strong demand for turbines used in AI data centers. Fastenal, an industrial supplies distributor, saw its shares outperform, driven by market-share gains and double-digit sales growth. The Communications sector outperformance was from Netflix (NFLX). Shares of Netflix gained after the announcement that the company would not acquire certain assets from Warner Bros. Discovery's (WBD) studio. Additional top contributors to 1Q26 performance included Walmart (WMT), Taiwan Semiconductor (TSM), and Microsoft (MSFT) with the Fund's underweight in Microsoft benefiting performance.

For the twelve months ending March 31, 2026, the Fund underperformed the Russell 1000 Growth Index. Allocation and selection detracted from performance. The Information Technology, Health Care, and Consumer Discretionary sectors were the top detractors from performance. ServiceNow (NOW), an enterprise IT software-management provider, was the top detractor over the twelve months ending March 31, 2026. ServiceNow shares, along with other software stocks, underperformed as advances in generative AI led investors to reassess the revenue and profit outlook for software companies. Visa also detracted from performance. Like ServiceNow, Visa (V) shares lagged amid concerns that new technologies may disrupt its business model and weigh on future revenue and profit growth. Worries about the potential use of stablecoins for payments and the possibility of agentic commerce changing how transactions are completed contributed to the weakness. Potential regulatory changes, including the proposed Credit Card Competition Act, also pressured the stock. Despite these concerns, neither ServiceNow nor Visa have reported meaningful changes in fundamental operating performance. Top contributors to performance for the twelve months ending March 31, 2026, include Alphabet (GOOG), Caterpillar, Taiwan Semiconductor, and not owing Mastercard (MA).

Portfolio outlook

Large-cap companies at the intersection of technology and the consumer continue to see rising expectations for sales and profit growth. Many large-cap companies in technology-driven sectors are increasing investment to support sales and profit growth initiatives. We believe companies that manage costs effectively while continuing to invest in growth can position themselves for a stronger profit outlook.

Our investment process continues along the same path with ongoing refinements. We view our regular meetings with the large-cap analysts as crucial to our process of researching existing names (operating developments) and considering new names to add to the Fund. In terms of refinements, we are spending more time on risk management and benchmark reviews which we anticipate will help us limit unintended exposures.

Fundamentally we believe investors will favor large-cap growth companies with strong organic growth opportunities, large and/or expanding target addressable markets, and operating initiatives that reflect data-driven insights to serve their end markets.

data.title: Madison Dividend Income Fund Q1 2026 Investment Strategy Letter
data.description:
For the first quarter, the Madison Dividend Income Fund (class I) returned +5.8%, which outperformed the S&P 500 Index, Russell 1000 Value Index, and Lipper Equity Income peer group returns of -4.3%, +2.1% and +0.2%, respectively.

Throughout 2025, we highlighted in our Dividend Income Investment Strategy Letters what we believed to be growing risks to the overall market due to the combination of S&P 500 valuations near all-time high levels and significant market concentration. Those risks began to play out in the first quarter as valuations contracted. According to FactSet, the expected forward price-to-earnings ratio declined from 22x entering the year down to 18x ending the quarter. Additionally, the eight largest stocks in the S&P 500 (Nvidia (NVDA), Apple (AAPL), Alphabet (GOOG), Microsoft (MSFT), Amazon (AMZN), Broadcom (AVGO), Meta (META), and Tesla (TSLA)), which represented 35% of the index, all underperformed. On a sector basis, the best performing sectors from the prior three years were among the worst performing sectors in the period. Technology, Communication Services, and Consumer Discretionary declined -9%, -9%, and -7%, respectively. These factors caused the S&P 500 to fall -4.4%.

The Dividend Income Fund was insulated from these risks due to its diversified portfolio of cheaper dividend stocks along with the fact it didn't own any of eight largest S&P 500 stocks. In terms of valuation, our preferred metric is looking at the relative yield of dividend stocks versus the S&P 500. Relative yield is defined as a stock or portfolio dividend yield divided by the S&P 500 dividend yield. A high relative yield compared to its long-term history indicates a low valuation for the stock or portfolio, while a low relative yield ratio indicates a high valuation, all else equal. At quarter-end, the relative yield of the Dividend Income Fund was 2.05x the S&P 500 and 1.35x the Russell 1000 Value Index, both at the high end of their historical ranges. In our view, dividend stocks remain on sale compared to both indexes.

Another factor driving Dividend Income outperformance was its large position in Energy, which was the best performing sector by a wide margin, as it gained +38% in the quarter. Notably, none of the other 10 S&P 500 sectors gained more than +10%. The Dividend Income Fund significantly increased its Energy holdings over the past six months by adding to existing positions and starting a new position in ConocoPhillips (COP). Energy increased from 9% of the portfolio on 9/30/2025 up to 16.5% as of 3/31/2026. We believe a new Energy bull market likely began in late 2025 that could continue to drive Energy stock outperformance.

Detracting from relative performance in the first quarter were several of our Financial stocks in what was a weak quarter for the sector.

While overall market valuation levels are cheaper following S&P 500 decline in the quarter, we continue to believe there are risks of further multiple contraction and a market correction. The Dividend Income Fund seeks to protect against these risks by owning a diversified portfolio of high-quality, above-average dividend stocks in cheaper areas of the market. We think Dividend Income has important defensive characteristics that help protect capital in down markets like was experienced in the quarter. These favorable attributes include:

  • An absolute portfolio dividend yield of 2.50%, which compares favorably to the absolute dividend yields of 1.2% for the S&P 500 and 1.85% for the Russell 1000 Value Index as of year-end.
  • A growing income stream that helps protect against inflation. Portfolio holdings have increased their dividends by 6% on average over the past year, which is well above inflation rates over that period. Consistent annual dividend increases are a key metric we require in portfolio holdings.
  • A high-quality portfolio with strong balance sheets that could protect on the downside in a market correction. 94% of fund holdings are rated A- or better by Standard & Poor's, which compares favorably to the S&P 500 at 35% and the Russell 1000 Value at 22%. If there is a down market and/or credit spreads begin to widen, we believe our high-quality portfolio is likely to hold up much better than the overall market.

The Madison Dividend Income Fund is a conservatively-managed equity strategy that owns a high-quality portfolio of above-average dividend yield stocks with strong balance sheets and sustainable competitive advantages (wide moats). We believe that owning high-quality stocks with above-average dividend yields is the best way to provide income and generate attractive long-term returns over a full market cycle while limiting drawdowns in bear markets and market corrections.

data.title: BP: The Market Still Underestimates This Turnaround
data.description:
Introduction

The last time I covered BP p.l.c. (BP), I rated them Buy, highlighting their smart decision to halt the buybacks as their balance sheet took priority amid the jump in stock price, with the stock returning about 26% since publishing it in February.

Following a solid quarter, BP is reiterated as a Buy given their long-term potential, the near-term Iran boost, and high hopes in their new CEO supporting their turnaround.

Turnaround Advances

BP reported a solid Q1, beating the market’s top- and bottom-line estimates, although the FCF was negative due to the timing of their working capital build, highlighting the seasonality of the company's inventory builds, being similar in terms of OCF to Q1’25.

Meanwhile, BP also announced the agreement to sell their Gelsenkirchen refinery, which they expect to increase their structural cost reduction target by $1 billion to a massive $6.5 billion to $7.5 billion by 2027, noting that they plan to reduce their hybrid bond financing by about $4.3 billion to $9 billion by the end of 2027, depending on the market conditions.

Due to seasonal maintenance predominantly in the Gulf of Mexico and the effects of disruption in the Middle East, BP expects its Q2 production to drop, while the reported upstream production would be lower in 2026 as a result of the ongoing Iran conflict, or broadly flat on an underlying level.

Regarding the CAPEX, they continue to expect between $13 billion and $13.5 billion (now evenly weighted throughout the year; below the $14.5 billion reported in 2025), expecting between $9 billion and $10 billion in proceeds from divestments this year ($6 billion from Castrol), most of which would come in H2'26, while the Deepwater Horizon would continue to drag them down, as highlighted before.

Financially, based on BP’s latest report, although we see an increase in net debt compared to Q4’25 (still down vs. Q1’25), it’s important to note that given the company’s significant ongoing divestitures and the renewed focus on their balance sheet over buybacks, we’re likely going to see this balance sheet improve significantly over the next couple of years. The company’s main target remains getting to a net debt of $14 billion to $18 billion by the end of 2027 compared to $22.2 billion as of their Q4 report, aiming at improving their credit rating within an A-grade range.

BP also declared a $0.4992 quarterly dividend per ADS, for a yield of roughly 4.30% at the moment of writing this, representing a bit over $5.1 billion and being well covered even if we consider last year’s $9.96 billion free cash flow, leaving plenty of room for them to focus on near-term business improvements before focusing more on growth or higher shareholder returns.

BP’s new CEO, Meg O’Neill, who officially took the helm on April 1, 2026, after her great performance at Woodside Energy (WDS), can also be a major tailwind for the company, with her legacy being really impressive, turning that one into a business with several very solid global operations that are going to fuel their growth for several years ahead, which can also be the case for BP given their portfolio rebalancing mentioned before.

Sure, there are also some shareholders that don’t necessarily want to see a massive swing back to oil and gas; the latest AGM came with over 50% of shareholders voting against the management's plan to ease climate reporting standards, and ~25% voted in favor of demanding more disclosure on capital discipline for fossil fuels.

Iran Boost May Be Temporary

On top of significant improvements in the market’s optimism surrounding BP’s potential turnaround, the Iran conflict offered an extra boost but also another level of risk that investors have to keep in mind.

Although war premiums are likely going to last for months (or more) after the conflict itself is over, that doesn’t mean the price will remain as elevated as today, even though the region’s energy infrastructure has been damaged, oil wells take a lot of effort to reopen once closed (if that’s going to be the case) - although that’s a far bigger issue in the US compared to the Middle East due to the geology - and a reopening of the Strait of Hormuz could be followed by a jump in demand, which combined can lead to a “higher-for-longer” price environment.

This is in line with the latest near-term outlook from the EIA, which sees higher oil prices now persisting into 2027, with significant differences between their February and April outlooks:

We now expect that Brent crude oil prices will increase from an average of $81/b in 1Q26 to a peak of $115/b in 2Q26 before gradually falling to an average of $88/b in 4Q26. We base this on the assumption that the conflict does not persist past April and that traffic through the Strait of Hormuz gradually resumes but does not return to pre-conflict levels until late 2026. Shut-in oil production gradually returns as flows through the strait resume and oil trade flows adjust. Given this relatively long adjustment period after flows through the strait resume, we expect oil prices will remain elevated, with Brent crude oil prices averaging $76/b in 2027, about $23/b higher than in our February STEO forecast.

Still, we should also consider the effect this event has on the global economy, with expectations of slower GDP growth globally combined with delayed rate cuts or even hikes in some regions.

This can lead to oil’s weak fundamentals from before taking over again once the Iran-driven shock is absorbed, with the potential to see OPEC hike production again to take advantage of the higher prices or more supply hitting the market due to higher near-term profits for oil and gas companies that can be reinvested.

Regarding OPEC - after what happened in Venezuela and Iran - we recently saw news of the UAE, the third-largest producer in OPEC, voluntarily leaving the organization on May 1 after more than 5 decades, which can be a major hit to its impact going forward, while the Iran conflict led to a 27% drop in OPEC's production in March, the biggest hit seen in recent decades. This can also come as a blow to Iran’s proposal to open Hormuz, with WTI jumping above $100 on the news alongside Trump’s negative tone regarding Iran’s plan.

Although a solid boost for the foreseeable future, I believe the Iran conflict and especially the market’s “hysteria” around it can be a threat for BP, its peers, and especially their near-term valuations, and we saw how all of these fell significantly back when the previous truce news came.

Still, given BP’s specific situation, this boost might’ve come at a very good point, potentially boosting the value the company manages to unlock from the assets they want to sell, as they’re technically still in a major asset recycling/divesting cycle. In this regard, I’ll also include the IEA Chief’s warning, who said that the world is facing its biggest energy security threat in history, calling it worse than 1973, 1979, and 2022 combined and stating that it has already changed the fossil fuel industry forever:

Their perception of risk and reliability will change. Governments will review their energy strategies. There will be a significant boost to renewables and nuclear power and a further shift towards a more electrified future.

Risks Reiterated

Although BP’s new CEO can be a major tailwind, and there’s a lot of potential for her to remodel this over the longer term, the Iran conflict adds near-term risk and opportunities we have to pay attention to.

Meanwhile, although this also comes with an opportunity for them to maximize the value they get from their asset sales, there’s still potential to see macro pressure affecting their pivot, while the company and its stock will ultimately always follow the price of the commodity.

Valuation

For BP’s DCF to Equity valuation, assuming oil prices similar to 2025 throughout the entire period (equivalent to a WTI oil price of about $65), given their guidance, lower CAPEX, cost savings, and portfolio adjustments, I’ll assume $12 billion in 2026, followed by a 5% CAGR until 2030, backed by their portfolio’s pivot and further cost/CAPEX reductions, followed by a 3.5% CAGR over the subsequent 5 years but also accounting for a $1 billion boost from 2033 related to their Deepwater Horizon payments dropping and another $300 million added back starting from 2034, with a 2.5% terminal growth rate.

Note that I’m assuming no change in the company’s net borrowings, as they should be able to repay their debt through the asset sales, and I want to highlight their cash generation potential.

To take into account their specific pivot-related and commodity risks, I’ll use a very high 12% discount rate to demand a strong margin of safety, with 2.582 billion ADS outstanding as of the end of Q1 (or ~15.5 billion for the regular share).

This way, we get to an equity value of $152.77 billion, for an intrinsic value of $9.86 for their regular share and $59.17 per ADS, which remains well above the current $46.57 at the moment of writing this. In other words, even if we assume much more conservative estimates, the current valuation continues to apply a significant margin of safety.

Conclusions

BP remains rated Buy, backed by significant portfolio adjustments and a potential near-term boost from Iran, while their new CEO has the credentials needed to bring them back to their former glory - or better.

There are certainly risks to keep in mind given the fact that we’re talking about a commodity company first and foremost, but the current valuation already prices in significant levels of risk, with solid potential to see the company deliver strong returns if their turnaround goes well.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Build-A-Bear Workshop: The Pros And Cons Of Owning The Stock Now
data.description:
Build-A-Bear Workshop, Inc. (BBW) achieved consistent top-line growth in the past few years by managing to turn around a stagnating business. Retail sales increased, international expansion with franchising also gained traction, and the firm's commercial segment also rapidly grew. The macroeconomic headwinds, however, cannot be disregarded - including consumer confidence levels, the tensions in the Middle East, as well as the elevated energy prices. The high short interest also raises concerns. My aim today is to give a balanced view of Build-A-Bear's business and bring you the key reasons why I believe that a "hold" rating is the best I can do at the moment.

Sales

Let me start with sales. Over the past three years, Build-A-Bear managed to increase its sales each year, with retail sales, commercial revenue, and international franchising all growing. There are several key points here, which I would like to highlight:

1. This demonstrates that Build-A-Bear is now a growing business and has a business model that does not only rely on direct-to-consumer sales, but they also managed to diversify their channels. This is largely thanks to the current CEO and her workaround strategy. The issue, however, is that she is set to depart this year, which always introduces some uncertainty about the future of the business.

2. I like that the firm is growing internationally. It is clearly visible from the net retail sales per square foot figures, but also from the amount and distribution of franchise locations. There is one key risk here, however, which I believe is important to consider. Build-A-Bear has 32 franchise locations in the Gulf States, including places like Kuwait, Qatar, and the UAE. Considering the ongoing tension in the region, demand may be soft for BBW's products in these countries in the coming months.

3. Looking forward, the firm does guide for revenue growth, mainly because of new store openings, but it also guides for potentially slightly declining margins. When it comes to topline growth, I generally like to see comparable growth increasing organically and not just by opening new stores.

Comparable growth, however, may face headwinds from consumer confidence and the ongoing tension in the Middle East, as highlighted above.

Further, on the cost side, there may be stronger headwinds than the firm initially accounted for. Tariff headwinds are one thing, which has been explicitly mentioned, but the higher oil prices may last for longer than initially expected. If so, both transportation and manufacturing costs can rise, potentially pressuring Build-A-Bear's margins.

Balance Sheet

Looking at the balance sheet, there are three line items that I would like to discuss. First, the accounts receivable. When talking about the sales, I always like to take a look at how receivables changed in the same period. Generally, accounts receivable changing at the same pace would be desirable. If receivables grow faster than sales, it could be a warning sign that the firm is selling more on credit, potentially pulling demand forward from future periods, or they have changed their accounting. The fact that Build-A-Bear's receivables grew by more than 30% in the prior year may be concerning at first, but it can be explained. The reason that receivables grew fast is a result of the growth of the commercial segment, in which revenue is recognized differently than in the direct-to-consumer business, therefore leading to higher receivables. Regardless, if accounts receivable grow faster than revenue, it still causes cash conversion cycles to lengthen, potentially reducing Build-A-Bear's financial flexibility.

Return to Shareholders

While Build-A-Bear has not been very consistent with paying regular quarterly dividends to their shareholders, they have been committed to returning value. Over the past five years, they used a combination of dividends and share buybacks to return value to investors. In general, I like to see consistency and growth because it allows investors to plan ahead with relatively low uncertainty if they are dependent on these regular payments. But I do understand Build-A-Bear's approach too. They seem to be making payments based on the cash flow generated and the capex used, meaning if they have a higher amount left to pay out, they do that in a relatively flexible manner by buying back shares. It does make planning ahead more uncertain, however. Some may also be concerned by the fact that they use more cash for share buybacks than for dividends; however, this is not an issue from my perspective, as buybacks may be treated in a more attractive way, from a tax point of view, in some jurisdictions.

All in all, I like that Build-A-Bear is committed to returning value to their shareholders, but I would be happier if their dividends or buybacks were more consistent, so planning ahead would be less uncertain.

Valuation

When it comes to the valuation, I do not believe that there is a huge opportunity to take advantage of here. Although Build-A-Bear is selling at a meaningful discount compared to the sector median, it seems to be trading about in line with its historic averages. At this moment, considering the firm's fundamentals and the macroeconomic landscape, I believe Build-A-Bear is fully valued, and some risks may not even be priced in yet, despite the strong price decline year-to-date. At this point, we also have to be aware that the short interest in the stock is almost 21%, which means that in case of a short squeeze, the share price can change rapidly.

All in all, I do not believe that a buy rating could be justified from a valuation point of view.

Conclusions

While Build-A-Bear is a growing business now, it mainly relies on franchising and new store openings to achieve growth. I would much rather see organic, comparable sales growth growing rapidly, driven by strong demand. The macro headwinds, however, are likely presenting this right now, especially consumer confidence levels and the ongoing tension in the Middle East.

The firm has remained committed to returning value to its shareholders; the payments were not really consistent, however. This makes planning ahead difficult, especially for investors, who rely on regular payments.

From a valuation point of view, I am also not too excited about Build-A-Bear, despite the fact that they trade at a discount compared to the sector median. The almost 21% short interest, in fact, raises significant concerns.

For these reasons, I rate Build-A-Bear as a hold now.

data.title: Cadence Design Systems: The Foundation For All Things AI
data.description:
Cadence Design Systems Overview

In late October last year, I initiated coverage on Cadence Design Systems, Inc. (CDNS) with a Buy rating. In that analysis, I found signs of robust demand and strong AI tailwinds. While the valuation wasn't cheap, I was bullish as the company played and continues to play a vital role in this technological era. Just earlier this month, the company announced deals with Google Cloud (GOOGL) and Nvidia (NVDA).

As you can see in the chart below, the stock saw a period of weakness after my article was published, but recently there has been a rather sharp rebound. Cadence reported their earnings just yesterday (April 27th) after market close, and so today I have decided to provide an update to see if the investment thesis has changed.

Below, it is shown that demand for Cadence's solutions continues to strengthen. Revenue growth is accelerating, and the backlog has hit a new record. While the fundamentals aren't perfect and the Hexagon deal may cause near-term profitability softness, the long-term outlook seems as strong as ever. With the company being the foundation for the AI revolution, I view the premium valuation as justified and the pullback in the forward P/E since late October as an opportunity to buy. Therefore, I've decided to reiterate my bullish view on Cadence stock.

Demand Remains Robust

Let's start today with a look at their business activity and demand metrics. Firstly, for the top line, Cadence reported revenues worth $1.474 billion. This represents a YoY growth rate of 19%, which is very respectable and is a considerable acceleration from the previous quarter's 6% increase in revenues. This shows strengthening business activity overall, and they also beat analyst expectations by over $18 million to show outperformance.

In their Q1, revenue mix by product category remained unchanged from the year-ago quarter as Core EDA continues to be the flagship offering of the company. In their earnings call, CEO Anirudh Devgan stated that:

As the industry begins transitioning to agentic AI, the need for physically accurate and highly mathematical EDA solutions becomes even more critical.

At this point, it seems that agentic design is becoming increasingly important as the company announced an expanded partnership with TSMC (TSM) just days ago. With agentic AI being a driver for EDA growth, I would say the outlook is strengthening.

Beyond the top line, the company's backlog really speaks volumes regarding how strong demand is right now. They reported that their backlog hit a fresh record of $8.0 billion, and that is up notably from the previous quarter's $7.8 billion and significantly from the prior year period's $6.4 billion. This shows that customer demand continues to pile up.

Furthermore, it should be noted that their cRPOs have increased both QoQ and YoY as well, and so some of this demand should show up on the top line in the not-so-distant future. As a whole, I believe there is little question that current business activity and demand are in favorable positions, with the AI revolution continuing to rely on their solutions for integrated circuit innovation.

Margins A Little Mixed

For Q1, there was a slight mix in how their margins fared, so let's take a look here. As you can see above, non-GAAP gross margin saw 40 basis points of deterioration YoY. Note that in the earnings call, CEO Devgan stated that "demand for our hardware accelerated in Q1, resulting in our best quarter ever" and so it seems that the dip in gross margin may be a result of the evolving software-to-hardware mix.

For costs and expenses, there were generally strong control measures. The $815 million non-GAAP figure represents a 13% increase YoY, which notably lags the top-line growth rate. This is a signal that the business has been overall efficient. This is also signaled in their operating margin. In non-GAAP terms, this key profitability metric expanded by 3 percentage points to 44.7% despite the dip in gross margin. As a whole, while the gross margin dip may not be the best news, I believe it's clear that Cadence remains a very well-run business.

The operating margin strength also trickled its way down to the bottom line. For Q1, the company reported an adjusted EPS of $1.96, up 25% YoY. This outpaces the top line growth rate, represents an acceleration of growth from Q4, and beats analyst expectations by $0.07. Therefore, I believe it would be fair to say that the profitability of their business is currently in fine shape.

Are Cash Flows A Concern?

While the common stock offering of 3.2 million shares back in late February was due to the acquisition of Hexagon AB's design and engineering business, there may be some developments in the company's cash flows and capital returns that are worth mentioning.

Firstly, despite the top and bottom line strength discussed above, operating cash flows dropped by 27% YoY to $356 million. If you take a look at their balance sheet, you'll see that they have net receivables of $1.034 billion as of the end of March. This is a whopping 78% jump YoY, and so the rate at which they are collecting receivables may be weaker than desired. In fact, days sales outstanding (DSO) have increased from 44 in the year-ago quarter to 67.

With CapEx up from the year-ago quarter, FCFs saw a 34% drop. Meanwhile, share repurchases are down YoY from $350 million to $200 million. While it's definitely not time to panic, I believe investors would be wise to keep an eye on their cash flow metrics to see if strength returns, since at the end of the day, cash is king.

2026 Financial Guidance

Cadence's updated outlook now includes the Hexagon design and engineering acquisition that I discussed thoroughly in my previous article. Nonetheless, their midpoint revenue estimate for FY2026 was above consensus expectations, while the adjusted EPS midpoint projection is in line with what analysts were expecting.

As you can see in the table above, revenue growth is now expected to accelerate from FY2025 levels, but it does seem that the Hexagon deal will have unfavorable effects on profitability, at least for the near term. While the top line will strengthen, you can see that non-GAAP operating margin expectations have been lowered and now imply contraction from FY2025.

Also, note that DSO has been revised much higher to 65, and so cash flow weakness may persist. In my previous article, I stated that the Hexagon acquisition may create new opportunities and synergies down the road, and so the short-term pain conveyed by the above guidance may be worth it.

CDNS Stock Valuation Isn't Overly Cheap

In the grand scheme of things, a forward P/E above 40 isn't the cheapest. Seeking Alpha shows that this multiple is at a nearly 80% premium to the IT sector. That said, the current reading of 40.4 is below levels seen in my previous article and also below the average of the past few years. Therefore, I believe there are reasons to believe that Cadence remains a Buy.

To be clear, Cadence is one of the few key companies that have powered the AI revolution beneath the surface. Many of the top chipmakers in the world today use their solutions to drive innovation. In today's article, it was shown that demand is basically at all-time highs, and while their fundamentals aren't perfect (gross margin weakness and cash flow declines), I would say that the company remains incredibly well positioned. Furthermore, the guidance above shows that the Hexagon deal will be a near-term drag on profitability, but at the same time, investors shouldn't forget about the "world-class simulation capabilities" this deal brings, as mentioned in my previous article. Therefore, I believe the long-term outlook for Cadence is as bright as ever right now.

With Cadence being the foundation for all things AI, a premium valuation isn't too surprising in my view. Furthermore, with the valuation now being lower than it was in late 2025 even after the recent rebound, I believe current levels are attractive enough for investors to buy.

Takeaway

My Buy rating for Cadence back in late October hasn't worked out the best, but after today's analysis, I would say that it would be a mistake to give up on the stock now. As stated above, the fundamentals and near-term outlook definitely aren't perfect, but at the end of the day, after the Hexagon deal, Cadence is in a stronger position than ever to provide chipmakers with the solutions that they need to innovate in this AI era. Given their place within the industry and the fact that the valuation is lower than in late 2025, I believe a reiteration of the buy rating would be appropriate now.

data.title: Natural Gas Recovers, But Downside Risks Remain
data.description:
By Anton Kharitonov

​Henry Hub is currently trading in a weak and relatively narrow range, with the front-month (May 2026, NGK26) holding around $2.50-2.57/MMBtu, with about $2.55 as the nearest short-term reference level. Over the past month the contract has lost roughly 15-20% from its March highs, highlighting pressure from inventories and mild spring weather rather than a clean, sustained downtrend.

The main bearish driver for the front-month is the storage structure: the latest weekly EIA injection of about 103 Bcf and total underground stocks around 2,063 Tcf keep the market in a structurally oversupplied state. This reinforces the seasonal negative bias, turns each EIA report into a trigger for front-month selling, and effectively locks in the “oversupplied spring” phase.

Weather remains the key short-term factor: a mild spring with moderate temperatures is limiting both heating and cooling demand, leaving the front-month in a range-bound regime within roughly $2.36-2.70 and highly sensitive to weather revisions and short-term headlines.

At the same time, the medium-term fundamentals look more balanced. Growing LNG exports to around 17 Bcf/d in 2026, along with further infrastructure expansion, are turning the U.S. into a net gas exporter, pulling volumes out of the domestic market and supporting higher prices further out the curve. Declining production and a concentrated stack of short positions add the risk of a short-covering rally, particularly if the weather deteriorates sharply or a geopolitical trigger appears.

Localized spot weakness, including negative prices in certain regions such as Texas, underscores structural overproduction and infrastructure constraints, but it does not alter the broader fundamental picture already baked into the futures curve.

Traders are increasingly favoring calendar spread strategies (long further-out months vs. short front-month) and positioning for short squeezes in the context of weak weather demand, while keeping short-term directional trades confined within the $2.36-2.70 band and focused on EIA reports and weather updates.

In effect, the current Henry Hub market reflects a clash of two time frames:

  • Fundamentally short-term bearish (front-month suppressed by high inventories and weak demand)
  • Fundamentally medium-term bullish (LNG exports, seasonal patterns, contango, and a structurally rising price profile along the curve)

The market does not look “broken” over the long run; instead, it appears to be passing through a low-cycle phase, after which the expectations of tighter supply and rising demand already embedded in the curve are positioned to materialize in the coming seasons.

The current rebound toward resistance near $2.60 may be used as a selling opportunity, but a confirmed break above that level would open the way for a move toward $2.70.

This material may contain third-party opinions; none of the data and information on this webpage constitutes investment advice according to our Disclaimer. While we adhere to strict Editorial Integrity, this post may contain references to products from our partners.
